I think the only way around that would be to have some ability to have certain channels configured (locally) as completely auto-update. So `juliaup` could be updated by `brew`, and if say the `release` channel is configured for auto-update, then when you update `juliaup`, the `release` channel would automatically also update to the latest Julia version in it.

If I understand correctly. `juliaup` installed via brew then updated via `brew up` will update the default Julia channel configured in `juliaup` ?

I am wondering if the warnings regarding using homebrew on the home page are no longer necessary?

","user":{"login":"musm","id":4319522,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzMTk1MjI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4319522?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm","html_url":"https://github.com/musm","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/musm/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":7746458376,"node_id":"LA_kwDONPNfxc8AAAABzbmTCA","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"d73a4a","default":true,"description":"Something isn't working"},{"id":7801806138,"node_id":"LA_kwDONPNfxc8AAAAB0QYdOg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/labels/linear%20algebra","name":"linear algebra","color":"f2b3db","default":false,"description":"Linear algebra"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2016-03-07T22:05:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:25:08Z","closed_at":"2016-03-09T22:14:08Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Reported by Tomas (see [https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/julia-users/BxWs1Vj_6Ng](url))

> Hello all,
> I was polishing my call and I have found the following definition of daxpy! I was not aware of
> 
> ``` julia
> function axpy!{Ti<:Integer,Tj<:Integer}(α, x::AbstractArray, rx::AbstractArray{Ti}, y::AbstractArray, ry::AbstractArray{Tj})
>     if length(x) != length(y)
>         throw(DimensionMismatch("x has length $(length(x)), but y has length $(length(y))"))
>     elseif minimum(rx) < 1 || maximum(rx) > length(x)
>         throw(BoundsError(x, rx))
>     elseif minimum(ry) < 1 || maximum(ry) > length(y)
>         throw(BoundsError(y, ry))
>     elseif length(rx) != length(ry)
>         throw(ArgumentError("rx has length $(length(rx)), but ry has length $(length(ry))"))
>     end
>     for i = 1:length(rx)
>         @inbounds y[ry[i]] += x[rx[i]]*α
>     end
>     y
> end
> 
> ```
> 
> Is the first check
>  length(x) != length(y)
> really an intended behavior? 
> 
> The multiplication goes over indexes rx and ry, should not be the check  length(rx) != length(ry) ?
> 
> Thanks for the clarification.
> Tomas

   and long drive letters are once more recognized. This has been
   broken since #19695 (Julia 0.5).

PR adds an additional workspace argument to most internal sorting functions and allows the end-user to pass one in with the keyword argument `workspace` (undocumented).

There are two types of workspaces: nothing, and an AbstractVector. nothing indicates that sorting functions should allocate new workspaces as needed and discard them when done. An AbstractVector indicates that sorting functions should use that vector, resizing it larger if needed. Wherever possible, sorting functions can handle receiving nothing, and AbstractVector with appropriate eltype, or no argument at all.

This PR also utilizes that feature to implement solution 3 of #45326 and therefore closes #45326.

As is, this PR exposes a new public-facing keyword argument for (almost) all sorting functions:
```julia
sort(x, workspace=y)

PR adds an additional workspace argument to most internal sorting functions and allows the end-user to pass one in with the keyword argument `workspace` (undocumented).

There are two types of workspaces: nothing, and an AbstractVector. nothing indicates that sorting functions should allocate new workspaces as needed and discard them when done. An AbstractVector indicates that sorting functions should use that vector, resizing it larger if needed. Wherever possible, sorting functions can handle receiving nothing, and AbstractVector with appropriate eltype, or no argument at all.

This PR also utilizes that feature to implement solution 3 of #45326 and therefore closes #45326.

As is, this PR exposes a new public-facing keyword argument for (almost) all sorting functions:
```julia
sort(x, workspace=y)
```

Some libraries using `getenv` may not see changes made by manipulating `ENV` in Julia. For example, the HDF5 library uses `getenv` on Windows.

Julia sets environment variables using `SetEnvironmentVariableW`:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/7ecbfa9c926bdf380f824766b9d165ec9b133eaa/base/env.jl#L23-L30

```julia
julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"]
"C:\\quantum\\powered\\plugins"

julia> unsafe_string(ccall(:getenv, Cstring, (Cstring,), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"))
"C:\\quantum\\powered\\plugins"

julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"] = raw"C:\plugins"
"C:\\plugins"

julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"]
"C:\\plugins"

julia> unsafe_string(ccall(:getenv, Cstring, (Cstring,), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"))
"C:\\quantum\\powered\\plugins"

julia> ccall(:_putenv_s, Cint, (Cstring,Cstring), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH", raw"C:\julia_plugins")
0

julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"]
"C:\\julia_plugins"

julia> unsafe_string(ccall(:getenv, Cstring, (Cstring,), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"))
"C:\\julia_plugins"

julia> ccall(:_wputenv_s, Cint, (Cwstring,Cwstring), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH", raw"C:\plugins\for\HDF5_jl")
0

julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"]
"C:\\plugins\\for\\HDF5_jl"

julia> unsafe_string(ccall(:getenv, Cstring, (Cstring,), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"))
"C:\\plugins\\for\\HDF5_jl"
```

To facilitate the use of Julia with the use of standard C (C99, C11) software on Windows, we should consider using `_wputenv_s` where possible or provide an alternative facility to facilitate interop.

# References (via [cppreference.com](https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/program/getenv))
C11 standard (ISO/IEC 9899:2011):
* 7.22.4.6 The getenv function (p: 352-353)
* K.3.6.2.1 The getenv_s function (p: 606-607)

C99 standard (ISO/IEC 9899:1999):
* 7.20.4.5 The getenv function (p: 317)

C89/C90 standard (ISO/IEC 9899:1990):
* 4.10.4.4 The getenv function

I found this comment insightful from the  linked  Python thread

> `_wputenv` keeps Python's copy of the environment block in sync with CRT's copy, which in turn calls `SetEnvironmentVariableW` to sync with process environment block. The CRT's copy of the environment excludes and disallows variable names that start with "=". Because the interpreter initializes `os.environ` from the CRT environment, Python has never included or allowed 'hidden' variables such as "=C:".

## Current Behavior Conda (4.5.12 and also after upgrading to 4.6.2) gets stuck on solving environment for a bioconda recipe `phyloflash`. The problem appears to be new. I first encountered it several days ago when trying to create a new environment containing the `phyloflash` recipe, and have since have heard reports from other users, and also reproduced the problem on a virtual machine with a fresh miniconda install. It was still able to solve the environment on 2019-01-08. Here is the head of the `conda-meta/history` log from that date: ``` ==> 2019-01-08 15:06:11 <== # cmd: /ebio/ag-swart/home/kbseah/anaconda3/bin/conda create --name phyloFlash phyloflash sortmerna=2.1b # conda version: 4.5.12 ``` ### Steps to Reproduce The following command gets stuck: ``` conda create -v -v -n pftest4 --dry-run phyloflash # stuck at solving environment` ``` This is the tail of the debug output: ``` DEBUG conda.common.logic:minimize(889): Final peak objective: 11 DEBUG conda.common.logic:_run_sat(734): Invoking SAT with clause count: 1099296 DEBUG conda.common.logic:_run_sat(734): Invoking SAT with clause count: 694156 DEBUG conda.common.logic:_run_sat(734): Invoking SAT with clause count: 473981 DEBUG conda.common.logic:_run_sat(734): Invoking SAT with clause count: 368343 DEBUG conda.common.logic:_run_sat(734): Invoking SAT with clause count: 423744 DEBUG conda.common.logic:_run_sat(734): Invoking SAT with clause count: 450666 DEBUG conda.common.logic:_run_sat(734): Invoking SAT with clause count: 436596 ``` Whereas it is able to solve the environment if I attempt to create an environment containing the dependencies specified in the bioconda recipe: ``` conda create -v -v -n pftest3 --dry-run "perl>=5.13.2" bbmap emirge pigz samtools vsearch bedtools mafft spades r-base r-ggdendro r-gtable r-reshape2 r-ggplot2 r-optparse # dry run successful ``` Following the suggestion in issue #8051, I removed `conda-forge` from the channels list and it was able to solve, suggesting that the issue lies there. While I can install the software with this workaround, it is not ideal because the Bioconda [docs](http://bioconda.github.io/) recommend giving conda-forge priority, so most users would have it set up like this. ## Expected Behavior Environment should solve as it did before. ## Environment Information

Windows"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2022-02-06T19:54:42Z","updated_at":"2022-02-09T15:20:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As previously noted in #11215, Windows tracks two environmental variable tables. Some libraries using `getenv` may not see changes made by manipulating `ENV` in Julia. For example, the HDF5 library uses `getenv` on Windows.

Julia sets environment variables using `SetEnvironmentVariableW`:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/7ecbfa9c926bdf380f824766b9d165ec9b133eaa/base/env.jl#L23-L30

```julia
julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"]
"C:\\quantum\\powered\\plugins"

julia> unsafe_string(ccall(:getenv, Cstring, (Cstring,), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"))
"C:\\quantum\\powered\\plugins"

julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"] = raw"C:\plugins"
"C:\\plugins"

julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"]
"C:\\plugins"

julia> unsafe_string(ccall(:getenv, Cstring, (Cstring,), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"))
"C:\\quantum\\powered\\plugins"

julia> ccall(:_putenv_s, Cint, (Cstring,Cstring), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH", raw"C:\julia_plugins")
0

julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"]
"C:\\julia_plugins"

julia> unsafe_string(ccall(:getenv, Cstring, (Cstring,), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"))
"C:\\julia_plugins"

julia> ccall(:_wputenv_s, Cint, (Cwstring,Cwstring), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH", raw"C:\plugins\for\HDF5_jl")
0

julia> ENV["HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"]
"C:\\plugins\\for\\HDF5_jl"

julia> unsafe_string(ccall(:getenv, Cstring, (Cstring,), "HDF5_PLUGIN_PATH"))
"C:\\plugins\\for\\HDF5_jl"
```

To facilitate the use of Julia with the use of standard C (C99, C11) software on Windows, we should consider using `_wputenv_s` where possible or provide an alternative facility to facilitate interop.

# References (via [cppreference.com](https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/program/getenv))
C11 standard (ISO/IEC 9899:2011):
* 7.22.4.6 The getenv function (p: 352-353)
* K.3.6.2.1 The getenv_s function (p: 606-607)

C99 standard (ISO/IEC 9899:1999):
* 7.20.4.5 The getenv function (p: 317)

C89/C90 standard (ISO/IEC 9899:1990):
* 4.10.4.4 The getenv function

Windows documentation
* [`_putenv_s`, and `_wputenv_s`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/reference/putenv-s-wputenv-s?view=msvc-170)
* [`_putenv` and `_wputenv`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/reference/putenv-wputenv?view=msvc-170)
* [`getenv` and `_wgetenv`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/reference/getenv-wgetenv?view=ms`vc-170)

(A quick and naive read of InteractiveUtils' `editless.jl` might suggest otherwise, as it [contains](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/bf534986/stdlib/InteractiveUtils/src/editless.jl#L136-L145) both the strings `code` and `code.exe`, but not `code.cmd` [unless you [go back in time](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/commit/d22c88fab)]).

Quite a time sink figuring this out. Hopefully adding this one line to the manual spares future Julia users the frustration.

explicitly states that objects of type [`String`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/strings/) or [`Symbol`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/base/#Core.Symbol) are immutable. For all intents and purposes, this is true. However, 
```julia
# version 1.7.0-rc3
julia> ismutable("string")
true
julia> ismutable(:symbol)
true
